text: lockdown, going long stretches without human contact. I honestly couldn’t think the way I usually do. I got stuck. Yes, I think it’s something new. Did you ever know anybody who got stuck like that? Their brain following the wrong track, and they couldn’t get off it? Oh, right. I guess you did. Gran. Gran and the Cabinet People. I’m sorry to bring it up. I thought I was just telling a funny story about my police encounter. Your idiot brother, right? Well, I don’t want to keep you. I’ll check back sometime next week, okay? In the meantime, Sis, as my officer buddy would say: stay out of trouble. # Hope you haven’t been worried about me, Mira. It’s been nearly a month since we talked. I gathered you didn’t want to get into it about Gran last time. That’s why I ended the call so abruptly. We should talk about her now, since I’m not sure we ever did. Not seriously. Is that okay? I’ll take your silence as a reluctant Yes. Listen, I know I didn’t react well when you told me about the Cabinet People. It’s just that I’ve known Gran since we were kids, and she was always sharp as a whip. We could never pull one over on her, right? Like that trick with the cereal bowl, or when I pretended to— Okay, you get the picture. So when you told me she was having hallucinations, I thought it was a temporary thing. Something she could be talked out of. We all listen to reason eventually? That’s what I was thinking at the time. She was fine whenever I spoke with her on the phone. Same old Gran. Older, sure, moving a bit slower so not catching me on the first few rings. But her mind was fine. So I’d been kind of dismissive of your concerns back then, which was unfair. You were the one who visited her the most—and later, you were generous enough to open your home to her and care for her. I mean, I couldn’t take her in , what with my job and my one-bedroom apartment. I felt guilty about that, and was always grateful that you stepped up. I guess I could have shown that gratitude more. Like, maybe, listening to you. Deferring to your judgment. Don’t get mad, but...I’d insisted so long that you’d been worrying too much, had my stubborn mind made up about it. To the point where, when I finally knew better, I couldn’t bring myself to admit it. I asked you not to get mad, Mira. I’ll say it now, okay? You were right. But it was just that one time. That’s how I explained it to myself after I saw Gran in her apartment. She was always asking me to visit, and I could never seem to find the time. During one of our phone calls, I let slip about a long weekend, my not having any special plans, and that’s all it took. She wouldn’t hang up until I promised to see her for Saturday dinner. The visit was great. Her second-floor apartment was just like I remembered it when we did sleepovers as kids—though Pops wasn’t sitting by the radiator, his chair aimed at the TV and a full ashtray balanced on one of the arms. Without the clouds of smoke he provided, the place was clean and fresh. Gran swizzled up a ham steak with a side of yams and brown-and-serve rolls, and everything was perfect. What’s Mira been going on about? Maybe my sister’s the crazy one. I can’t lie. That’s what went through my head at the time. Gran drizzled some Hersey’s syrup over store-brand vanilla ice cream, and we enjoyed dessert in the den. She mentioned how I always had a sweet tooth. “I often think of you sitting right there,” pointing to the same spot I occupied on the couch, “stirring cookie batter, so proud at how careful you were being, since you kids were supposedly too messy to bake anything at home.” It was a sweet memory of a time that was precious to me, and more so because Gran remembered it so clearly. She gave my knee an affectionate tap, leaned closer, and I waited for her to share another wonderful moment from my childhood. “There are people living in my cabinets,” she whispered, like someone telling a ghost story, or admitting a private shame. Her eyes moved from my face to the kitchen and back. To the kitchen where she’d just baked dinner. A wary, terrified gaze at the same cabinet where she’d retrieved the can of chocolate syrup. My heart sank. She’d made me a kid again, with that nostalgic dinner. Affectionate memories had been rushing back in an overwhelming torrent. In the afterglow of dinner and ice cream, I couldn’t separate from my younger self—the kid who loved his grandmother, and candy, but also had that sense of wonder, who believed in ghosts or other monsters, who could look at an ordinary kitchen cabinet and imagine evil beings contorted behind thin wooden doors. The cabinets didn’t lock. Long fingernails would scratch at the wood, push the doors slightly open to let dark, deep-set eyes peer through the gap. A drop of saliva drips from blood-hungry mouths; teeth gnash in anticipation. I was too afraid to look toward the cabinets. But a lot could happen when I wasn’t looking. I thought I heard the shift of glass jars, ceramic saucers and bowls. The creak of hinges. I stared at Gran’s face instead, her eyes wide behind thick glasses. She heard what I was hearing. She’s been living with this fear every minute of the day. And night, too. Because who knows what Cabinet People could do at night, without watchful eyes to keep them in their place? Gran’s hand was on my knee again, and she squeezed tight. “When they use the toilets,” she said, “they don’t flush afterwards.” Well,
